coredata.dc:title: Real-time lithium plating onset detection for lithium-ion batteries via dynamic impedance spectra analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Xinghao
Meng, Jinhao
Amirat, Yassine
Gao, Fei
Benbouzid, Mohamed
coredata.dc:description: Real-time lithium plating detection is essential for ensuring lithium-ion batteries’ safety and longevity. While impedance analysis offers valuable insights into the lithium plating process, the plating detection efficacy is often limited by overlapping electrochemical-thermal phenomena, complicating the extraction of plating-specific impedance features. To overcome this challenge, this work proposes a dynamic impedance tracking framework based on a specifically designed equivalent circuit model (ECM), enabling real-time observation of interfacial electrochemical dynamics. An adaptive lithium plating detection framework further enhances accuracy by employing statistical thresholding to distinguish plating-induced impedance variations from normal operational fluctuations. A consistency metric is formulated to quantitatively assess the proposed method’s performance across diverse charging rates and thermal conditions. Experimental validation demonstrates the proposed method’s superior sensitivity and robustness compared to three conventional impedance-based plating indicators.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Lithium plating
Dynamic impedance

head.ce:title: Real-time lithium plating onset detection for lithium-ion batteries via dynamic impedance spectra analysis

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Innovation Platform (Center) for Industry-Education Integration of Energy Storage Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Real-time lithium plating detection is essential for ensuring lithium-ion batteries’ safety and longevity. While impedance analysis offers valuable insights into the lithium plating process, the plating detection efficacy is often limited by overlapping electrochemical-thermal phenomena, complicating the extraction of plating-specific impedance features. To overcome this challenge, this work proposes a dynamic impedance tracking framework based on a specifically designed equivalent circuit model (ECM), enabling real-time observation of interfacial electrochemical dynamics. An adaptive lithium plating detection framework further enhances accuracy by employing statistical thresholding to distinguish plating-induced impedance variations from normal operational fluctuations. A consistency metric is formulated to quantitatively assess the proposed method’s performance across diverse charging rates and thermal conditions. Experimental validation demonstrates the proposed method’s superior sensitivity and robustness compared to three conventional impedance-based plating indicators.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), known for their high energy and power density, long service life, and lack of memory effect, have become the primary energy storage components in electric vehicles (EVs) and energy storage systems (ESSs). However, with the widespread adoption of LIBs, concerns about their safety and reliability have been increasing [1,2].

Among the various failure risks in LIBs, lithium plating has attracted significant attention. This phenomenon occurs when lithium ions (Li
+
) are reduced to form metallic lithium, which deposits on the anode surface. The deposited lithium can further develop into dendritic formations that penetrate the separator, increasing the risk of internal short circuits [3]. In addition, lithium plating results in the irreversible loss of active lithium, which contributes to capacity loss and accelerates the battery aging during repeated charging and discharging cycles [4,5]. The likelihood of lithium plating is much higher under circumstances such as fast charging, low temperature, high state of charge (SOC), or overcharging. In these scenarios, the slow diffusion of Li
+
and the elevated anode overpotential create an environment conducive to lithium plating [6,7].

Extensive research has been conducted to understand lithium plating mechanisms, and various plating detection techniques have been developed to mitigate its adverse effects. Post-mortem characterization methods, such as scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), have been employed to investigate the morphological and structural changes of batteries [8–10]. For instance, Zhang et al. [9] employed SEM and XPS to identify inhomogeneous lithium plating near electrode edges, which contributes to increased internal resistance and capacity loss. Fang et al. [10] introduced an ex-situ 7Li NMR methodology to separately quantify lithium plating and solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) formation in graphite electrodes. However, these methods are inherently destructive, requiring battery disassembly and material extraction, which limit their applicability.

To facilitate lithium plating detection in real time, thermal, thickness, and acoustic measurements can help reflect the lithium plating state. For instance, Carter et al. [11] investigated lithium plating behavior under thermally transient charging conditions. It was found that rapid temperature drops during charging induce substantial lithium deposition at the anode. Grimsmann et al. [12] developed a high-resolution inductance sensor to detect lithium plating by measuring subtle changes in cell thickness. It was demonstrated that excessive charging currents lead to an irreversible increase in cell thickness. Xie et al. [13] used spatially resolved deformation detection and ultrasonic diagnostics to investigate lithium plating in large-format LIBs. Ding et al. [14] applied synchrotron-based soft X-ray microscopy for the identification of lithium stripping. Different stripping modes that contribute to dead lithium formation were characterized. While these methods help understand the lithium plating mechanism and process, there remain difficulties in reliably identifying the onset of lithium plating, particularly in practical battery management systems.

To meet onboard requirements, cell voltage measurements during the charging and relaxation process have been explored for lithium plating detection. For example, Chen et al. [15] applied incremental capacity (IC) analysis to investigate the lithium plating process. It is found that a distinct IC peak corresponds to the lithium plating onset. However, IC peaks also shift due to temperature variations and battery degradation, potentially leading to false diagnoses. Schindler et al. [16] introduced the voltage relaxation profile for lithium plating detection. It is demonstrated that plated lithium gradually dissolves during rest, leading to distinct two-step voltage recovery profiles. Konz et al. [17] applied differential open-circuit voltage (dOCV) analysis to detect lithium plating during fast charging. It is shown that dOCV features correlate with the severity of plating. However, the dOCV analysis cannot be performed in real time, as it requires a long resting period after charging.

To address these limitations, dynamic impedance monitoring provides real-time tracking of the plating process [18,19]. For example, Koseoglou et al. [20] measured the impedance from 3.2 kHz to 0.2 Hz for lithium plating detection. It is demonstrated that the plating events are marked by a significant decrease in interfacial impedance. Similarly, Wang et al. [21] proposed an impedance trend identification method, showing that a sudden drop in the real part of the impedance at 1 Hz serves as an effective indicator of plating onset. Shen et al. [22] integrated impedance analysis at 1 Hz with long short-term memory (LSTM) to detect abnormal drops in charge-transfer resistance during multi-stage fast charging. Straßer et al. [23] further explored the impedance-based detection, finding that while low-frequency measurements (e.g., 2.18 Hz) exhibit greater impedance variation between normal and plated conditions, the impedance at 488 Hz provides a distinct separation between nominal behavior and plating effects. However, practical challenges persist in implementing dynamic impedance for lithium plating onset detection. Specifically, the impedance characteristics vary significantly during the battery charging process [24], reducing the reliability of fixed-frequency indicators. Also, the detection sensitivity is often hindered by overlapping electrochemical and thermal effects, which obscure plating-specific impedance features [25]. The overlapping processes include the charge transfer reactions related to lithium intercalation at the anode and the diffusion of lithium ions within the electrode and electrolyte [26]. Consequently, advanced analytical methods are required to isolate the impedance signatures associated with lithium plating and improve detection accuracy.

Prior research highlights significant limitations in existing methods for detecting lithium plating. Post-mortem techniques, while offering detailed analysis of plating morphology and lithium deposition mechanisms, are inherently destructive and thus impractical for real-time monitoring. In-situ methods, which rely on physical, acoustic, and electrical signals to deduce the plating process, struggle to reliably pinpoint the onset of plating, particularly in real-time applications. Impedance-based approaches, despite their potential for real-time detection, are hindered by overlapping electrochemical processes, complicating the isolation of impedance variations specifically linked to lithium plating.

To bridge these research gaps, this work proposes a real-time lithium plating onset detection framework based on dynamic impedance spectrum analysis. The primary contributions of this work are as follows:
1.
A dynamic impedance tracking framework is established using a specifically designed equivalent circuit model (ECM). The ECM captures key interfacial and diffusion processes, and its parameters are continuously updated to enable high-resolution, real-time monitoring of battery dynamics throughout the entire charging cycle.
2.
An adaptive lithium plating detection framework is proposed to identify abnormal drops in charge-transfer resistance, effectively distinguishing plating-induced impedance variations from normal operational fluctuations through statistical thresholding.
3.
A consistency metric is developed to quantitatively assess the performance of different lithium plating indicators under varying charging rates and thermal conditions.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section II presents the dynamic impedance measurement. Section III details the impedance modeling and lithium plating detection method. Section IV presents the experimental validation. Section V concludes the paper.

LIBs exhibit nonlinear and time-varying behavior during charging, primarily due to complex electrochemical-thermal interactions. To detect the subtle changes indicative of lithium plating, the dynamic impedance measurements are presented to characterize the evolving battery dynamics. To reduce the complexity of dynamic impedance calculations, we assume that the impedance remains approximately stationary over short intervals [27]. The dynamic impedance Z ( ω , t ) over a selected interval t ∈ [ 0 , T ] is expressed as:
(1)
u ( t ) =
u d
( t ) +
F
− 1
{ Z ( ω , t ) I ( ω ) } +
u n
( t )
where u ( t ) is the measured voltage,
u d
( t ) slowly varying drift component,
F
− 1
denotes the inverse Fourier transform, and
u n
( t ) accounts for noise and nonlinear distortions.

The polarization-induced voltage drift component
u d
( t ) , influenced by factors such as SOC and temperature, masks the frequency-dependent behavior and complicates impedance analysis [28]. To isolate the impedance response,
u d
( t ) is extracted using a moving average filter expressed as:
(2)
u d
[ m ] =
1 N
∑
l = m − N
/
2
m + N
/
2 − 1
u [ l ]
where
u d
[ m ] and
u [ l ]
are the discrete forms of
u d
( t ) and u ( t ) , respectively; N denotes the number of voltage measurements throughout one perturbation cycle.

The voltage response signals can be obtained by subtracting the drifting components from the raw voltage measurement, expressed as:
(3)
u
p t
[ m ] = u [ m ] −
u d
[ m ]
where
u
p t
[ m ] denotes the perturbation-induced voltage response signal in discrete form.

To analyze the battery impedance responses at
t k
, i and
u
p t
are transformed into the frequency domain using the discrete Fourier transform (DFT) expressed as:
(4)
{
I

ω n
) =
∑
m = 0

]

− j

U

ω n
) =
∑
m = 0

u
p t
[ m +
f s

− j

/
f s
where
I
(

ω n
)
and
U
(
t k

ω n
)
denote the current and voltage harmonics at the time
t k
and the frequency
ω n
.
f s
represents the sampling frequency. By performing Ohm’s law, the dynamic battery impedance
Z
(
t k

ω n
) is determined by:
(5)
Z
(

,
ω n
) =
U
(

ω n
)
/
I
(
t k
,
ω n
)

Dynamic impedance measurements capture the electrochemical behaviors of LIBs across varying time scales. However, key processes, such as lithium intercalation and ion diffusion, exhibit frequency ranges that overlap with the impedance characteristics of lithium plating. To isolate parameters that reflect lithium plating characteristics, the impedance modeling approach is analyzed.

The battery impedance spectrum is divided into three distinct frequency domains: high-frequency (HF), medium-frequency (MF), and low-frequency (LF), each corresponding to specific electrochemical processes within the cell. To effectively analyze and interpret impedance variations across these frequency ranges, this study employs an ECM. The corresponding Nyquist plot and ECM representation are illustrated in Figs. 1 (a1) and 1(b1). The proposed ECM assumes a linear, small-signal response and models a time-variant system where selected parameters evolve to reflect changes in battery state during operation. Specifically, the inductance L and ohmic resistance
R 0
are treated as time-invariant due to their relative stability, while all other parameters are allowed to vary with time to capture dynamic electrochemical behavior during the battery charging process.

In the HF region, the impedance response is predominantly influenced by the inductive effects of current collectors and external wiring, represented by the inductance component L in the ECM. The ohmic resistance
R 0
accounts for the intrinsic resistances of the electrolyte and electrodes. The MF region captures the effects of charge-transfer kinetics, which evolve dynamically during the charging process. This region is characterized by the charge-transfer resistance
R
c t
and the behavior of the electrochemical double layer at the electrode-electrolyte interface. To incorporate dispersion effects, the double layer is modeled using a constant phase element (
CPE 1
). At low frequencies, the impedance behavior is dictated by solid-state diffusion, which governs the slow transport of Li
+
within the electrode material. While the Warburg element is conventionally used to model ideal semi-infinite diffusion, real batteries exhibit non-ideal diffusion due to electrode porosity and surface heterogeneity. To account for these deviations, the diffusion-related impedance is modeled using
CPE 2
instead of a Warburg element.

In low-temperature environments or degraded cells, an additional semicircle may appear in the HF region, attributed to the emergence of a SEI layer. However, this study focuses on lithium plating detection in LiFePO4 cells, where the SEI contribution to impedance is negligible and does not significantly affect the Nyquist plot [29]. Consequently, its effect is excluded from the ECM to simplify the modeling process.

The impedance of ECM at the frequency
ω n
is calculated by:
(6)
Z
model
(
p
k
,
ω n
)
=
R 0
+ j
ω n
L +
R
c t
1 +
R
c t
( j
ω n
)
N 1
Y 1
+
1
( j
ω n
)
N 2
Y 2
,
p
k
= [ L ,
R 0
,
R
c t
,
Y 1
,
N 1
,
Y 2
,
N 2
]
where
p
k
represents the parameter vector;
N 1
and
N 2
represent the dispersion coefficients of
CPE 1
and
CPE 2
, respectively, which range from 0 to 1;
Y 1
and
Y
2
represent the generalized capacitances of
CPE 1
and
CPE 2
, respectively.

The ECM parameters are determined by fitting the model impedance
Z
m o d e l
(
p
k
,
ω n
)
with the measured impedance
Z
(
t k
,
ω n
)
. To estimate these parameters, this study uses the Nelder-Mead algorithm [30], which iteratively minimizes the following objective function:
(7)
{
C
(
p
k
) =
∑
n = 1
M
|
Z
(
t k
,
ω n
) −
Z
m o d e l
(
p
k
,
ω n
)
|
2
s . t .
p
min
≤
p
k
≤
p
max
where
p
min
and
p
max
define the lower and upper bounds; M represents the total number of impedance measurements.

The simplified electrochemical processes occurring on the graphite particle surface are presented in Figs. 1(c1) and 1(c2), where
I
ch
represents the battery charging current,
I
intercalation
and
I
plating
represent the lithium intercalation and plating currents, respectively. Lithium plating typically occurs during fast charging or low-temperature conditions, where the transport of Li
+
into the anode becomes kinetically constrained. Under normal charging conditions (Fig. 1(c1)), Li
+
intercalate into the graphite layers while electrons (
e −
) flow through the external circuit. However, during lithium plating (Fig. 1(c2)), excess lithium deposits on the anode surface instead of intercalating, leading to dendrite formation, accelerated degradation, and irreversible capacity loss due to active Li
+
depletion [4,31].

The occurrence of lithium plating is reflected in the Nyquist plot of impedance spectra (Fig. 1(a2)), where the shrinkage of the MF semicircle indicates the emergence of a parallel current pathway. The impact of lithium plating on ECM is illustrated in Figs. 1(b1) and 1(b2). Under normal charging conditions (Fig. 1(b1)), the charge-transfer process is governed by
R
c t
. When lithium plating occurs (Fig. 1(b2)), an additional plating-induced charge-transfer resistance
R
c t , p l a t i n g
appears in parallel with
R
c t
, reducing the overall charge-transfer resistance and altering the impedance response compared to normal intercalation behavior [20–22]. Meanwhile, the reduction in
R
c t
is linked to the increase in polarization and long-term capacity fade, since plated lithium reacts irreversibly with the electrolyte, further increasing internal resistance over cycles. Consequently, the abnormal drop of
R
c t
can be used to indicate lithium plating during the battery charging process. Algorithm 1 Workflow of the Proposed Method
The workflow of the proposed lithium plating onset detection method is summarized in Algorithm 1, which consists of three primary parts detailed as follows:

Part 1: Parameter update (lines 3–5). The ECM parameter vector is updated by fitting
Z
(
t k
,
ω n
) with the ECM, as shown in Eq. (7). To reduce the computational cost, the parameter bounds are dynamically adjusted based on the values from the previous iteration, expressed as:
(8)
p
min
=
p
k
−
r
⊙
p
k
,
p
max
=
p
k
+
r
⊙
p
k
where
r
represents the scaling factor that regulates the range of parameter updates, ensuring stability and convergence during optimization. ⊙ represents the element-wise multiplication operator. As previously discussed, the inductance L and ohmic resistance
R 0
are assumed to be time-invariant due to their relative stability and are therefore excluded from the update process by setting their corresponding scaling factors in
r
to zero.

Part 2: Smoothing and differencing (lines 6–12). Due to fluctuations in impedance measurements, direct analysis of
R
c t , k
alone cannot reliably detect the onset of lithium plating. To enhance robustness, an exponential moving average (EMA) technique is applied to smooth
R
c t , k
and reduce the influence of transient variations. The smoothing process is expressed as:
(9)
R ~
c t , k
= α ⋅
R
c t , k
+ ( 1 − α ) ⋅
R ~
c t , k − 1
where
R ~
c t , k
represents the smoothed
R
c t , k
, and α is the smoothing factor. To detect abnormal impedance variations,
R ~
c t , k
is differentiated over a lag of L , capturing dynamic changes in the charge-transfer process.

Part 3: Adaptive lithium plating detection (lines 13–21). Conventional diagnostic methods relying on fixed thresholding are limited by various operational fluctuations, which frequently trigger false positives. To accurately detect lithium plating, a statistical thresholding approach based on the Z-score is employed. To distinguish normal operational fluctuations from plating-induced resistance drops, a rolling mean ( μ ) and standard deviation ( σ ) are computed over a window w , providing an adaptive threshold:
(10)
μ =
1 w
∑
i = k − w + 1
k
δ
R
c t , i
,
σ =
1 w
∑
i = k − w + 1
k
(
δ
R
c t , i
− μ
)
2
where δ
R
c t , i
represents the differential of
R ~
c t , k
. The onset of lithium plating is detected when δ
R
c t , k
falls significantly below the expected range, as expressed by:
(11)
δ
R
c t , k
< μ − κ ⋅ σ
where κ serves as a sensitivity parameter, balancing false positives against detection responsiveness. In this study, κ is set to 2, which is a widely adopted threshold for identifying abnormal trends in statistical anomaly detection [32]. This selection establishes a 95 % confidence interval under Gaussian assumptions, limiting the false alarm rate to approximately 2.5 % while preserving rapid detection capability. L and w are set to 5 and 20, respectively, to balance sensitivity to changes in
R ~
c t , k
while reducing the impact of transient fluctuations.

When the condition in Eq. (11) is met, the plating indicator
A k
is set to 1, marking an abnormal drop in
R ~
c t , k
and suggesting the occurrence of lithium plating.

The experimental studies are carried out on a cylindrical 14,500 battery with a 600 mAh capacity. The cathode material is LiFePO4, while the anode material is graphite. As depicted in Fig. 2 , the experimental testing platform features a Howland current pump powered by a 60 W switching power supply, enabling the generation of customized waveforms. A data acquisition (DAQ) module, with a maximum sampling rate of 250 kHz, is integrated into the system to generate control signals and collect the voltage and current measurement data. A host computer is used for transmitting and storing data, and a thermal chamber is employed to regulate the ambient temperature during the battery testing.

In this work, a multi-frequency perturbation signal is applied to the battery charging current to extract the dynamic impedance spectra. The optimization framework presented in [33] constructs a binary sequence that integrates the most effective frequency components. This sequence provides several advantages, including high frequency-response accuracy, ease of implementation, and strong robustness against noise disturbances. The optimized binary sequence is thus employed in this work to perform dynamic impedance measurements. The perturbation signal spans a logarithmic frequency range from
f
min
= 0.1
Hz to
f
max
= 1.5
kHz , comprising 30 discrete frequency components. The signal period and amplitude are set to 10 s and 0.2 C, respectively. The characteristics of the perturbation signal in both time and frequency domains are illustrated in Fig. 3 .

To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method for real-time lithium plating detection under different circumstances, the battery is charged at different current rates and under various temperature conditions, as listed in Table 1 . Given that lithium plating typically occurs at lower temperatures, the experimental tests are conducted at 5
∘
C, 15
∘
C, and 25
∘
C. Each test begins with a full discharge at a 1 C rate until the lower cut-off voltage of 2 V is reached, followed by a two-hour relaxation period to allow the battery to reach equilibrium. The battery is then fully charged at the designated current rates until it reaches the upper cut-off voltage of 3.65 V. Throughout the charging process, perturbation signals are continuously injected as current disturbances superimposed on the charging current to track the dynamic impedance responses. After charging, the battery undergoes an additional one-hour relaxation period to record the relaxation voltage profile.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Validation of the proposed method at 25
∘
C
ce:section.ce:para: To track dynamic impedance spectra during charging, perturbation signals are superimposed onto the charging current, as shown in Fig. 4 . The impedance response is continuously updated at 1 % SOC interval, ensuring the balance between real-time detection capability and computational efficiency. The dynamic impedance measurements obtained from 10 % SOC to 90 % SOC are presented in Fig. 5 (a). To ensure the accuracy and validity of these impedance measurements, the Kramers-Kronig (K-K) relations are used for consistency verification. In the K-K tests, the oscillation parameter is maintained within the range of 0.8 to 1.0, following the validation criteria established in [34]. As depicted in Fig. 5(b), the maximum absolute impedance residuals remain below 0.5 %, confirming the high precision and dependability of measured dynamic impedance spectra. The impedance spectra shown in Fig. 5(a) demonstrate a distinct SOC-dependent variation, reflecting changes in the charge-transfer process and diffusion properties as the battery transitions through different SOC levels. Specifically, as SOC increases, ion transport efficiency improves and electrode kinetics are enhanced, leading to a reduction in the semicircle size of MF.

The charge-transfer resistance (
R
c t
) is extracted and continuously updated by fitting the dynamic impedance measurements using the ECM. As detailed in Table 2 , the parameter bounds
p
max
and
p
min
are chosen to be sufficiently broad to encompass the impedance spectra across various operating conditions [35]. The initial parameter vector
p
0
is set between
p
max
and
p
min
. To regulate the range of parameter updates, the scaling factor
r
is determined based on the observed impedance variation trends throughout the charging process. As illustrated in Fig. 5(a), HF impedance responses are primarily governed by ohmic resistance and inductive effects, which remain relatively stable throughout charging. By contrast, MF and LF impedance components exhibit significant variation due to changes in
R
c t
and diffusion behavior. Given these trends, the scaling factors are set to 0 for L and
R 0
(as they remain largely unchanged), while the remaining scaling factors are set to 0.2 to allow adaptive updates based on impedance evolution.

Fig. 6 presents the experimental results for
R
c t
identification and lithium plating detection. In Fig. 6(a),
R
c t
varies dynamically, with an EMA smoothing parameter of α = 0.3 applied to reduce fluctuation and enhance trend clarity. It is shown that the smoothed curve (dashed orange line) presents a monotonic decrease in
R
c t
with increasing SOC. At low SOC levels,
R
c t
decreases rapidly due to the increased concentration of Li
+
in the electrolyte. As SOC increases, the decrease rate in
R
c t
slows as the graphite anode becomes increasingly saturated with Li
+
. This leads to reduced availability of intercalation sites and increases the activation energy for Li
+
insertion [36]. However, at high SOC levels, an abrupt and abnormal drop in
R
c t
is observed. This can be explained by excess lithium deposits on the anode surface instead of intercalating. When lithium plating occurs, the emergence of the parallel current pathway leads to an abnormal drop in charge-transfer resistance [20]. By monitoring this resistance profile, the onset of lithium plating can be effectively identified.

In Fig. 6(b), lithium plating detection is performed using a differential impedance analysis approach, where the rate of change in
R
c t
, denoted as δ
R
c t
, is analyzed statistically. A statistical thresholding method is used, employing the rolling mean ( μ ) and standard deviation ( σ ) of δ
R
c t
to detect anomalous impedance drops. The inset figure illustrates the probability distribution of δ
R
c t
. A sharp decline δ
R
c t
is detected at 87 % SOC, indicating the onset of lithium plating, which is marked by a red triangular marker in the shaded region. The detection result is consistent with the plating mechanism, where lithium deposition occurs at high SOCs due to excessive local lithium-ion concentration and electrode overpotential.

The performance of the proposed method is compared to the real-time detection method with different lithium plating indicators, as shown in Fig. 7 . Specifically, the real impedance at 1 Hz, denoted as
Z
1~Hz
′
[21,22], at 488 Hz, denoted as
Z
488Hz
′
[23], and the MF semicircle size in the Nyquist plot, denoted as
Z
tr
′
−
Z
min
′
[20], are evaluated using the same statistical thresholding method for consistency. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the proposed method reveals a clear trend where the SOC at which lithium plating initiates decreases as the charging rate increases. This behavior coincides with the theoretical analysis, as higher charging rates lead to higher lithium-ion concentration gradients. Consequently, lithium plating occurs at lower SOC levels [4]. In contrast,
Z
1~Hz
′
and
Z
488Hz
′
exhibit large variations across SOC. However, there is a lack of a consistent drop at the plating onset. Likewise, the
Z
tr
′
−
Z
min
′
profile demonstrates inconsistent detection performance. The observed impedance fluctuations do not reliably correlate with plating onset, leading to unreliable diagnoses. While these indicators reflect general impedance evolution during charging, their sensitivity to lithium plating is limited due to the overlapping influence of electrode kinetics and ion transport properties.

Furthermore, the differential relaxation voltage profile after charging is utilized as an independent metric for lithium plating detection [16,17]. As shown in Fig. 7(a), a distinct two-stage voltage plateau emerges when a significant amount of lithium plating occurs at high charging rates (above 2 C), indicating significant lithium plating. However, at lower charging rates, this plateau is not quite obvious, even when plating occurs, due to the rapid re-intercalation of plated lithium. This indicates that while relaxation voltage analysis remains a valuable diagnostic tool, its sensitivity is limited compared to impedance-based detection [21]. Meanwhile, the application of the differential relaxation voltage method requires extended resting periods post-charging. In comparison, our proposed framework operates in real time during the battery charging process, making it suitable for practical battery management applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the proposed method under different circumstances
ce:section.ce:para:
To further evaluate the robustness of the proposed lithium plating detection method, experiments were conducted at different temperatures (15
∘
C and 5
∘
C) under various charging rates, as illustrated in Fig. 8 . As expected, lower temperatures accelerate lithium plating onset due to the reduced lithium-ion diffusion rate and high charge-transfer limitations [31]. As shown in Figs. 8(a1) and 8(a2), the differential voltage plateau becomes more pronounced at lower temperatures and higher charging rates, indicating more severe lithium plating under these conditions.

Comparing Figs. 8(b1) and 8(b2), the lithium plating onset occurs at a significantly lower SOC at 5
∘
C, even for moderate C-rates. This behavior can be explained by the higher anode overpotential at lower temperatures, which promotes lithium deposition. The proposed method reliably detects the plating onset, showing a distinct drop in
R
c t
across different C-rates. As seen in Figs. 8(c1), 8(d1), and 8(d2), impedance indicators at fixed frequencies exhibit inconsistent performance. It is difficult to distinguish impedance changes caused by lithium plating from other operational fluctuations. Similarly, Figs. 8(e1) and 8(e2) demonstrate that the diagnostic performance of
Z
tr
′
−
Z
min
′
proves unreliable under different temperatures and charging rates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of the detection results
ce:section.ce:para:
To quantitatively analyze the detection results with different plating indicators, we define a consistency rate ( C R ) expressed as:
(12)
C R =
c o u n t
(
S
1
)
c o u n t
(
S
1
) +
c o u n t
(
S
2
)
× 100
%
where the function
c o u n t
( ) denotes the number of elements in a set.
S
1
and
S
2
represent consistent and inconsistent detection sets, respectively. A detection result is classified into
S
1
only if it satisfies the following conditions:
(13)
{
S O
C
p t
(
C i
,
T i
) > S O
C
p t
(
C
i + 1
,
T i
)
S O
C
p t
(
C i
,
T i
) < S O
C
p t
(
C i
,
T
i + 1
)
where S O
C
p t
(
C i
,
T i
) represents the estimated SOC at the onset of lithium plating;
C i
and
C
i + 1
denote the charging current rates, where
C i
<
C
i + 1
.
T i
and
T
i + 1
denote the testing temperatures, where
T i
<
T
i + 1
. The detection result is considered inconsistent and assigned to
S
2
if it violates either of the above conditions.

C R quantifies the reliability of the lithium plating onset detection results. A high C R indicates that the detection method consistently captures the theoretical physical behavior of lithium plating, specifically, the trend that the onset SOC for lithium plating decreases with higher charging rates and lower temperatures [4,20]. As listed in Table. 3 , the proposed charge-transfer resistance tracking method achieves a C R of 100 %, demonstrating excellent alignment with the theoretical trends of lithium plating mechanisms. In contrast,
Z
1~Hz
′
and
Z
tr
′
−
Z
min
′
show moderate consistency ( C R = 64 %), suggesting that these indicators are less reliable under certain conditions. Meanwhile,
Z
488~Hz
′
performs poorly ( C R = 18 %), primarily due to its limited sensitivity in lithium plating detection. These results confirm the superiority and robustness of the proposed method for accurate lithium plating onset detection.

This paper proposes a real-time lithium plating detection framework based on dynamic impedance analysis. The dynamic impedance tracking framework is developed to track the charge-transfer resistance during battery charging, enabling high-resolution tracking of interfacial electrochemical processes. An adaptive statistical thresholding method is introduced to detect abnormal drops in charge-transfer resistance. The plating-induced impedance variations can be effectively distinguished from normal operational fluctuations.

The experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method. K-K consistency tests are conducted to ensure the reliability of the measured impedance spectra, where the absolute impedance residuals are limited to below 0.5 %. The lithium plating detection results align with theoretical physical trends across different charging rates and thermal conditions. The proposed method has consistent detection performance and outperforms three traditional impedance-based indicators.

Future work will investigate the feasibility of the proposed method for a wider temperature range and different battery chemistries. Additionally, this study will explore the scalability of the method and its long-term performance under actual operating conditions.

Xinghao Du: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Jinhao Meng: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yassine Amirat: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Fei Gao: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Mohamed Benbouzid: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
